coredata.dc:title: Experimental and numerical analysis of heat and gas generation during thermal runaway in NMC811 lithium-ion batteries under thermal abuse and inert conditions

coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion Batteries (LIBs) are essential to advancing renewable energy technologies; however, there are significant safety challenges, particularly concerning battery thermal runaway. Addressing these risks necessitates a deeper understanding of Battery Thermal Runaway (BTR) mechanisms, which has driven extensive research employing experimental and numerical methods. This study focuses on the thermal runaway behaviour of lithium-ion batteries, specifically those using NMC811 and NCA cathode chemistries. The thermal runaway has been experimentally characterized using an Accelerating Rate Calorimeter (ARC) in an inert environment. The experimental data have been used to validate a proposed model incorporating three-dimensional thermal modelling and thermal runaway chemical kinetics, emulating the Heat, Wait, and Seek methodology alongside ARC thermal abuse tests. The proposed model accurately predicts heat transfer phenomena within a closed-volume canister, estimating heat release, gas generation rates, and vented gas composition during thermal runaway events. These predictions are essential for enhancing safety assessments, improving battery design, and developing numerical models that predict LIB behaviour under thermal abuse conditions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion Batteries (LIBs) are essential to advancing renewable energy technologies; however, there are significant safety challenges, particularly concerning battery thermal runaway. Addressing these risks necessitates a deeper understanding of Battery Thermal Runaway (BTR) mechanisms, which has driven extensive research employing experimental and numerical methods. This study focuses on the thermal runaway behaviour of lithium-ion batteries, specifically those using NMC811 and NCA cathode chemistries. The thermal runaway has been experimentally characterized using an Accelerating Rate Calorimeter (ARC) in an inert environment. The experimental data have been used to validate a proposed model incorporating three-dimensional thermal modelling and thermal runaway chemical kinetics, emulating the Heat, Wait, and Seek methodology alongside ARC thermal abuse tests. The proposed model accurately predicts heat transfer phenomena within a closed-volume canister, estimating heat release, gas generation rates, and vented gas composition during thermal runaway events. These predictions are essential for enhancing safety assessments, improving battery design, and developing numerical models that predict LIB behaviour under thermal abuse conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are crucial in the world’s transition to renewable energy sources. Due to exhausted research and technological development, new chemical compositions, geometrical layouts, and integration of new systems have appeared in recent years. However, lithium-ion batteries still present some challenges to researchers and manufacturers, such as their safety hazards [1]. Battery thermal runaway (BTR) is an unstoppable exothermic reaction produced by lithium-ion batteries performing out of their Safety Operating Area (SOA) [2]. During this process, the cell’s temperature drastically increases, and flammable gases are ejected, leading to fire or explosions in severe cases [3–5]. Therefore, to improve the safety of this technology it is necessary to focus research efforts on understanding BTR.

Several studies in literature have pushed for an understanding of the BTR by implementing experimental evaluation methodologies and numerical tools. Some authors focused on understanding the thermal stability of the materials that compound the LIBs. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) are standard methodologies used to analyse the thermal stability of the battery component after the cell’s disassembly, allowing the identification of the chemical reactions that occur during BTR [6–8]. Other approaches have been proposed, like laser irradiation to quantify the minimum energy required to induce thermal runaway; in that sense, a controlled energy source impacts the cell’s casing, generating local heating and, consequently, the BTR [9–12].

In recent years, Accelerating Rate Calorimetry (ARC) has been established as the standard method for evaluating the thermal stability of commercial lithium-ion battery cells [13,14]. It's a thermal analysis technique that studies exothermic reactions and thermal stability by measuring the rate of temperature increase under pseudo-adiabatic conditions [15,16]. ARC offers several advantages for assessing BTR; one significant advantage is its precise thermal runaway onset temperature detection, providing crucial data for safety assessments and design improvements [17,18]. Additionally, ARC provides detailed information on the heat generation rate, enabling a comprehensive understanding of the battery's behaviour under thermal abuse [19,20]. Accelerating rate calorimeters also allow the implementation of close volume vessels to keep the thermal runaway products as gases and particles for further composition analysis and to evaluate the ejected mass flow rate of vented gases [21]. Different authors have used thermal abuse inside the canisters to characterise the products of thermal runaway combustion or the vented gases by adding inert gases [22,23]. Some studies have found that the main venting gas composition corresponds to CO, CO2, H2, CH4, and different hydrocarbons; this composition may vary depending on the cell chemistry, battery state of charge (SoC), and environment composition [24,24].

Developing models has also been a priority of the research community in order to obtain tools capable of reproducing the behaviour from experimental testing. First steps taken towards developing a BTR numerical model were taken by Hatchard et al. [25] in which the reactions kinetics for a LiCoO2 LIB exposed to a high temperature environment were presented. Additionally, it allowed assessing the effect of different materials on BTR. Kim et al. [26] pushed forward the previous work by extending it to a three-dimensional approach, allowing the model to capture hot-spot propagation due to thermal abuse tests and assess the effects of the orthotropic thermal conductivities of the employed LIB. Other authors have contributed with BTR kinetic models for different cathode chemistries, such as NMC111 [28,27], NCA [28], or other formulations for LCO chemistries [29]. Efforts have also been placed in predicting the pressure buildup inside the LIB when subjected to abuse scenarios, the safety cap opening and the venting of flammable gases, in that sense, Ostanek et al. [30] presented the appropriate equations to capture the pressure contribution of electrolyte and generated gases within a LIB. Additionally, under Non Vapour Liquid Equilibrium (NVLE) conditions (i.e. once the venting cap opened) they could predict the venting gas velocity into the domain, reaching venting internal pressures close to 20 bar. That approach has been employed by other authors [31,32] for their respective studies in which the combustion of ejected gases was considered.

The usage of advanced experimental techniques for thermal runaway characterisation is key for the generation of relevant data to develop numerical methods that predict the behaviour of the thermal event [33]. Different authors have used the combined methodology of DSC-ARC to propose additional kinetics models that integrate single and multi-step reactions to predict the species composition and the heat generation during the thermal runaway [36,34–36]. Kinetics models using DSC-TGA data have also been implemented to predict the batteries' internal pressure and venting process during the thermal runaway [37–40]. Additionally, to complement the venting process modelling, authors have proposed models to estimate the vented gas composition during the thermal runway based on kinetic chemical models [41].

Nonetheless, the current study implements experimental and numerical methodologies to deepen the understanding of the thermal runaway behaviour of lithium-ion batteries with NMC811 and NCA as cathode chemistry. These cathode chemistries have been selected based on their extensive use within the Li-Ion batteries [42] and their safety risk [43,47].

This paper presents a novel digital twin of the experimental setup used for battery thermal runaway characterization. The work enclosed aims to provide the scientific community a computational tool capable of reproducing the TR and venting event for widely used LIBs, that it can be later employed to characterize situations where experimental tests find difficult to reach, such analysis at module/pack level. Compared to the state-of-the-art and reviewed literature, this work further validates the kinetics models through comprehensive integration and experimental validation under different chemistries and conditions. Notably, the novel study employs a closed-volume environment inside an Accelerating Rate Calorimeter (ARC), using nitrogen (N₂) as the environment gas to isolate gas reactions from the thermal runaway during model validation. The Accelerating Rate Calorimeter with a closed-volume canister model integrates three-dimensional thermal modeling and thermal runaway chemical kinetics, allowing for simulation of thermal abuse tests and a deeper understanding of the various processes involved in battery thermal runaway: gas species generation, venting, and the unstoppable exothermic reaction. The model’s integration has been validated through Heat, Wait, and Seek (HWS) thermal abuse tests of commercial cylindrical cells. Additionally, the proposed model accurately predicts the heat transfer phenomena inside the closed-volume canister implemented in the ARC, providing a distinct advantage in understanding the thermal runaway behavior of the battery cell by estimating the heat release during the process. On the one hand, such a model will reduce the need for time-consuming experiments. On the other hand, the insights extracted from the validated numerical model will be directly applicable to real-world thermal runaway conditions in future studies.
The current study evaluates 18,650 commercial cylindrical cells with cathode chemistry NMC811 and NCA lithium-ion cells. The specific characteristics of each cell are described in Table 1 .

Both battery cells have been analysed using different methodologies to characterise the chemical composition of the different cell components. The cathode of LG 18650mh1 has been identified as 81.8 % of nickel, 11.8 % of manganese, and 6.4 % of cobalt using EDX (Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy) and ICP (Inductively Coupled Plasma) technique. Furthermore, different physic-chemical methodologies such as GC–MS, NMR, and FTIR (Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy) have been used to analyse the electrolyte composition of a pristine cell. It was found that the main electrolyte composition corresponds to 51.39 % of Dimethyl Carbonate (DMC), 41.59 % of Ethyl Carbonate (EC), and 7.02 % of Propylene Carbonate (PC), including Lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) as a lithium salt. Finally, ICP and TGA (Thermogravimetric analysis) has shown that the anode composition corresponds to 98 % graphite, and the binder present in both electrodes is Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF). The cell Sanyo NCR18650GA has been analysed using the same techniques, identifying its cathode composition as NCA and anode as graphite. Additionally, it has been found that the electrolyte is made by a 1:3 ratio of EC and DMC, respectably, including EMC and MPC as additives. Sanyo NCR18659GA cell includes Lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) as a lithium salt.

The experimental setup used for the thermal runaway evaluation in the current study is shown in Fig. 1 . The thermal abuse testing was conducted using the ARC THT EV+ accelerating rate calorimeter from Thermal Hazards Technologies. This equipment features electrical resistances on its top, side, and bottom surfaces, which act as pseudo-adiabatic barriers and heat the internal environment of the chamber. This allows the ARC to operate at a controlled temperature with a precision of 0.2 %. The ARC performs under the Heat, Wait & Seek method. During the test, the equipment pre-heats the cell to 50 °C and them in defined steps of 10 °C, then waits 40 min for the thermal stabilisation and seeks exothermic reactions for 20 min. If the ARC goes into the exothermal mode, it mimics the cell's temperature; in another case, it will continue the heating process. The ARC stops heating the cell once its temperature exceeds 300 °C for safety reasons; no cooling system is activated during this test.

The ARC is equipped with highly sensitive N-type thermocouples, offering a resolution of 0.001 °C and an accuracy of 0.7 % to precisely monitor the ambient temperature and detect thermal phenomena like exothermic reactions within the cell. The thermocouples are fixed to the cell’s surface by using metallic clamps, so superficial contact is guaranteed, and the measuring errors are avoided. In addition, the Yokogawa dl580v data logger records temperature variations in multiple positions within the cell, including the venting gases cap, the cell surroundings, and the canister walls.

A 19.5-litre hermetic cylindrical vessel, also known as the test canister, is specifically designed to support the battery thermal runaway phenomena within the ARC system. This vessel ensures that all the product gases from the battery thermal runaway are contained within the closed volume of the ARC. The test chamber can withstand pressures up to 40 bar without risk to the equipment. The canister is outfitted with six thermocouples to monitor the temperature of the cell and the surrounding air. Additionally, it features an absolute pressure sensor capable of measuring up to 100 bar with a precision of 0,5 % of the range and an acquisition rate of 10 Hz. Additionally, the canister features a scape pipe for gas extraction venting. An Agilent Micro GC 990 is used to quantify moles of the different species in the gas sample. The gas chromatographer has been calibrated using standardized bottles of CO2, CO, H2, CH4, C2H4, C2H6, and C3H6. The gas selections have been made according to the availability of literature on gas composition [44] and lab calibration bottles.

At the start of the test, the thermal runaway canister is filled with an inert environment of 99 % nitrogen at 3 bars. The hazard event characterization under inert conditions allows the isolation of the thermal runaway process from a potential reaction between the vented gases and a reactive gas in the environment, which can result in the combustion of the vented gases, generating different sub-products such as CO2 and H2O. Under inert conditions, the gas reaction between the vented gases and the environment can be ignored, as there is no oxidizer for the combustion reactions to occur. This allows for a clearer understanding of the battery's thermal runaway behavior, which is one of the main objectives of this study. However, the findings from the research do not rule out the possibility of applying real-case scenarios by incorporating a kinetic model to simulate the reactions between the reactive environment and the vented gases; this is proposed to be addressed in future work works. It is important to clarify that one of the main objectives of this study is the validation of the inert experimental methodology to validate the kinetic reactions happening inside the battery casing and not outside in the gas phase.
To guarantee the confidence in the measured parameters, the uncertainty values of the experimental equipment is shown in Table 2 .

The computational domain has been reproduced within the chosen CFD software STARCCM+ 2306. It comprises the closed volume chamber, the LIB holder, and the LIB itself. A representation of the geometry considered for the simulations has been included in Fig. 2 , in which the Closed Volume Chamber (Canister), the battery holder and the LIB are present. Due to the axisymmetric nature of the geometry, and to save computational cost, a two-dimensional surface is considered out of the circumference and axisymmetric condition has been imposed at its axis.

The domain has been spatially discretized into sub-elements in which the conservation equations that rule the phenomenon are solved. For the fluid phase (that is, the air contained within the volume and surrounding the LIB), a characteristic base cell size of 5 mm has been chosen for such discretization, and further refinement has been set in nearby regions of solid-gas interfaces. In that sense, prismatic layers are introduced in those boundaries (within gaseous domain). In that sense, a non-dimensional wall distance y +∼1 has been achieved to capture accurately heat transfer as the k-w SST [45] turbulence model is being employed. The total thickness of such prismatic layer has been set to reach a y +∼2000, indicating that it is sufficiently far away from the boundary layer. Regarding the solid regions, the cell sizes has been set to obtain at least 2 elements on the most restrictive sections to maintain certain spatial resolution on such sections. A mesh independence study has been carried out to ensure that the chosen base cell size will properly capture the heat transfer from the canister walls to the LIB, ranging with element sizes that result in cell counts of 9 × 103, 18 × 103 and 32 × 103. The temperature evolution of the cell was tracked as the value of interest and to ensure that the heat transfer from the canister walls to the LIB was captured, as well as the exothermic process of the LIB itself. From it, the original 5 mm base cell size was chosen, with the cell count of 18 × 103. The resulting spatial discretization has been included in Fig. 3 .

The LIB geometry considered is a 18,650 casing format made of aluminium with a wall thickness of 0.2 mm. The jelly-roll has been defined as a cylindrical mass located within the LIB casing with its characteristic properties in Table 1. Technical characteristics of lithium-ion cells used for experimental testing. The venting hole geometry has been simplified into a circular hole with an diameter with an area comparable to the one measured experimentally. In that way, the numerical model can be simplified, avoiding introducing complex shapes that could increase the computational cost. The use of the specified LIB format has been done so that the pressure increase achieved within the closed-volume walls due to the increase of temperature and the ejection of mass lies within acceptable safety limits. Nonetheless, the model, which will be provided below, will be applicable to other formats if the cathode chemistry remains the same, and the reactive mass is provided, as the proposed model will provide heat per unit of mass.

For the present work, a three dimensional TR model is employed, hence the generated heat from Eq. (1) is introduced into the heat transfer equation for solid conduction, Eq. TR kinetics available from literature have been extracted and used for the two LIB chemistries of interest. For the NMC811 cells, the proposed model from Ren et al. [36] has been employed. The proposed model was originally obtained for NMC111 cells, so to adapt it to the LIB with larger Nickel proportion, it has been modified to show an accurate replication. That appropriate modifications have been adopted based on a zero-dimensional model through the TRKIN tool from García et al. [34] under a HWS procedure. The original parameters from the model and the adapted ones for NMC811 chemistry is indicated in Table 3 . Those modifications have been done based on the assessment of the energy released by the different reactions. On them, it has been observed that the original model did not trigger TR. An initial study was performed to analyse at which temperatures the internal reactions took place. It was observed that the Cathode-Anode reaction was responsible of rising the temperatures from the final anode-reaction temperatures to the highly exothermic ones (Binder and Cathode). As in the original model this reaction was not providing sufficient heat to trigger TR, its pre-exponential factor was increase to facilitate this heat release. By the modification of the Cathode-Anode reaction (as indicated in Table 3). Such modifications have been done based on a 0-dimensional model shown in Fig. 4 . In that model, the default mechanism from Ren et al., and the modification of the mechanism to replicate the NMC811 behavior. The corresponding closed volume temperature is imposed as a boundary condition, and the temperature of the LIB is obtained. As observed, the introduced modifications on the TR model results in an accurate replication of the temperature curve, with a temperature difference of 32 °C compared to the experimental value. This temperature deviation lies within observed deviations in other computational studies, who show differences up to 50 °C [46]. On the other hand, the default model, with such boundary conditions do not trigger TR, achieving a maximum temperature of 150 °C, compared to the 815 °C of the experimental data. The rest of parameters have been set as per literature values in the original work by Ren et al. [36]. On the other hand, for the NCA LIB, the Kondo et al. [28] mechanism has been employed and no tuning of the provided parameters was needed (Table 4 ).

The thermal abuse of the LIB not only leads to exothermic reactions but also to the generation of certain gases associated to the degradation of the internal components. The main degradation pathways are indicated in Table 5 . For example, the SEI decomposition leads to generation of CO2 and C2H4. This carbon dioxide could further reduce promoting the generation of CO by its combination with lithium. The reaction between the electrolyte and the anode could lead to generating CH4 and C2H6. The experimentally-obtained results of gas composition and ejected mass of the LIB are introduced into the CFD model. Ejection of CO, CO2, H2, CH4, C2H4 and C2H6 has been considered in the study. The generation of gases due to the degradation of the internal material can be divided in two distinct phases. First one is characterized by a Vapour Liquid Equilibrium (VLE) of the electrolyte material, and it is considered while the safety cap remains closed. This takes place while the internal pressure of the cell does not overcomes the physical limit the cap can sustain. During VLE, the pressure contribution due to the electrolyte vaporization is given by Antoine equation (Eq. (11)).

The A,B,C parameters are dependent on the electrolyte composition. For this work, a mixture of 70 % Dimethyl Carbonate (DMC), 30 % Ethylene Carbonate (EC) is assumed for both NMC811 and NCA chemistries, and their values are given in Table 6 .

The generation of gases due to internal reactions, on the other hand is given by Eq. (12), which is calculated for each of the gases considered, based on the variation of the concentration of each degradation mechanism. The pressure contribution of them during VLE is given by the ideal gas equation of the species. This internal pressure dynamics model provides a reliable representation of its evolution, as shown in an article focusing on these internal dynamics by García et al. [51] in which it has been validated.

As the fluid phase has been considered during the completeness of the simulation, the Navier-Stokes equations need to be introduced into the model. For that, the conservation of mass (Eq. (19)), momentum (Eq. (20)), energy (Eq. (21)) and species (Eq. (22)) are considered. Turbulence plays an important role in the mixing of the ejected gases with the surrounding N2 and in the heat transfer throughout the gas. For that reason, a Reynolds-Averaged Navier Stokes (RANS) approach has been followed, closing the transport equations through the k-w SST turbulence model [45] (Eqs. (23)–(24)). In these equations,

Due to the wide range of temporal scales that take place during the heating and BTR process, and the long HWS procedure, a dynamic time-step procedure has been implemented.

The boundary conditions for the complete computational framework can be summarized as a temperature condition at the outer wall of the closed-volume chamber and a velocity inlet at the upper region of the LIB. Regarding the outer-wall temperature, it has been set to reproduce the temporal temperature profile of that region during the experiments. Once the time in which the experiments indicated that the “Seek” event was triggered, the input temperature was set to the same temperature of the LIB, hence achieving an adiabatic environment and recreating the experimental behavior. Regarding the inlet of the TR gases, the velocity and temperature calculated by Eq. (14) and Eq. (16) was imposed.

As mentioned, this study evaluates the BTR behaviour of two different chemistry 18,650 cylindrical cells, NMC811 and NCA. The thermal event characterisation was performed three times per cell; the test started at 50 °C and 3 bar, with an environment of nitrogen at a minimum concentration of 99 %; the chromatograph validated the nitrogen concentration before starting each test. The selection of a 3 bar environment was made to facilitate the sweeping of the environment in the canister and to remove the possible concentration of oxygen, as well as to improve the sampling of gases later during gas extraction.

The experimental measurement of the cell’s temperature and chamber’s pressure for each test is shown in Fig. 6 . In both plots it can be identified that the cells with cathode chemistry NCA reach the thermal runaway earlier than the cells with NMC811 as the active material of its cathode. This delay can slightly impact the onset temperature of the thermal event; during the test procedure, the average onset temperature fund for NCA is 128.9 °C while 130.6 °C for NMC811 cells. Additionally, it can be identified that both cells reach similar maximum temperatures during the thermal runaway; the NCA cells have reached an average maximum temperature of 654.3 °C while the NMC811 cells are 670.3 °C. From the ARC test, the venting temperature is, on average, 125.6 °C for NMC811 cells and 122.6 °C for NCA cells. The experimental results about the maximum temperatures meet different results from the literature. Shen et al. studied the thermal runaway characteristics of different NMC batteries with an N2 environment, finding maximum temperatures of 718.47 °C in NMC811 [52], while Golubkev et al. reached 678 °C for NMC chemistries [53]. Garcia et al. compared thermal runaway events in different chemistries in N2 environment, finding a reduction of around 50 °C in the maximum temperatures of the thermal event [54], hence explaining the lower achieved maximum temperatures.

The exothermic reactions found in the HWS test are shown in Fig. 7 ; this figure resumes the cell’s temperature, the chamber’s pressure, and the cell’s temperature rate only when an exothermic reaction is detected inside the ARC. From the temperature and the pressure plot, it can be identified how the time delay of the thermal event seems bigger if only the exothermic zone is analysed. However, this behaviour is common in ARC testing due to the equipment uncertainties and has been present in other authors’ research. Consequently, the present study is based on thermal parameters instead of time-triggered events. From the (c) plot can be identified the first phase of heating when the dT/dt is unstable and the exothermic reactions start, and then a sudden increase of the temperature and heating rates, which defines the beginning of the thermal runaway.

After the thermal abuse test, the vented gas remains at a close volume inside the ARC. Gas chromatography is used to analyse the gas, and the composition measured in each test is represented in Fig. 8 . From the figure, it can identify two main differences in the gas composition of the cells with different cathode chemistries. One of the differences found between the composition of the vented gases is that NMC gases present presented more CO2 than CO which could mean that NCA gases present a leaner combustion during the thermal runaway process, that means that the flammable gases released don’t have enough reactant for a proper combustion. However, the fraction of hydrocarbons present in the final gas composition of NMC cells is greater than the fraction in NCA cells, leading to the supposition that NMC cells release more hydrocarbons mass than NCA during the process and additionally this gas completes its reactions to form the combustion products.

The experimental results previously obtained have been employed for validation purposes of the computational model. In that way, the procedure of the ARCHWS protocol could be replicated. The temperature obtained at the canister wall has been imposed as a boundary condition to replicate the heating effect of the ARC walls during the HWS procedure. Once the simulation detects temperature gradients of the jelly-roll greater than 0.1 °C/min, the canister boundary conditions is then changed to follow-up the temperature of the same jelly-roll to replicate the adiabatic heating process, until BTR takes place. On the other hand, the final gas concentration obtained through the chromatograph results, in addition to the calculated gas phase mass lost, has been introduced into the gas generation mechanism model. Therefore, the solid material ejected is ignored, assigning to the model only the mass that has been lost in gaseous form. Due to the variability of these results, the average mass loss corresponding to the gas phase has been considered, established experimentally through the collection of solid ejecta, post-mortem analysis of the battery, and gas phase governing equations. Due to the nitrogen atmosphere, and no consideration of oxygen in the degradation reactions, no combustion model is included.

Fig. 9 shows the contours of the solution of the simulated NMC811 cell during the TR event for a specific snapshot. In it, it can be observed how the kinetics of the TR exothermic process are coupled to the gas generation mechanism. Also, it provides insight on the spatial discretization is preserved on the exothermic process, as it can be observed how it is the core of the jelly-roll which reaches greater temperatures. Regarding the gaseous phase, the presence of the jet can be visualized both by the temperature field and the velocity field, promoting the heating up and pressure buildup of the air within the closed volume. Regarding the internal pressure of the LIB, it has a slight higher value than the obtained within the closed-volume chamber, allowing the ejection of the produced gases.

The temperature profile of the LIB during the HWS protocol is first compared against experimental means. Fig. 10 shows the temperature of the LIB of interest under HWS for the three experimental tests carried out, and the outcomes from the simulations. Temperature evolution during the initial heating phase (0 - 300 min) is well captured, also in the HWS heating phase (300 min – onwards). Slight differences appear on the exothermic phase of the simulations. Experimental curves show a sudden increase in heating rate that simulations are not capable of reproducing, as they show a more progressive increase in temperature until TR start. Simulation results on NCA predict a TR event within the statistical dispersion of the experimentally tested LIBs, together with a maximum temperature peak close to experimental data, resulting in a satisfactory result from the simulation model. Regarding the NMC cell, similar outcomes can be extracted. In this case, the TR event is well captured within the experimental curves, which in this case depict a considerably lower statistical dispersion. Maximum temperature is over-predicted across the three experimental tests, showing variations in 100 °C for the NMC-2 and NMC-3 tests, and of 20 °C for the NMC-1 curve. Nonetheless, in overall, the CFD model returns satisfactory results across both chemistries.

Further validation is carried out on the temperature derivative obtained for both the experimental and computational methods, shown in Fig. 11 . In overall terms, the computational model replicates the experimental behavior once the TR is taking place (above 200 °C). Differences shown at lower temperatures are associated to differences in the heating process during the heat-wait-seek phase. Results for NCA cell, although reaching similar maximum temperature derivatives, initially heat up in a slower way, as observed in the temperature evolution (Fig. 10), as experimental results show a sudden increase of temperature in a short amount of time. This is attributed to the melting of the separator which induce the internal short circuit in the battery cell, accelerating the heat release process during the event, as mentioned by different authors [55,56]. On the computational side, due to the nature of the Arrhenius-like expressions this behaviour cannot be replicated. Regarding results of NMC cathode chemistry, this later phenomenon also takes place, although the differences are not as evident, later replicating the overall temperature derivative values until the maximum temperatures reached by the cells.

Although there is a lack of experimental data on the internal pressure of the cells, the model aims to replicate the evolution of that parameter during the simulations performed. Fig. 12 compares the predicted internal pressure with the temperature evolution of the LIB. The two curves clearly represent the VLE and NVLE events. The initial pressure as observed is at 1 bar, and with increasing temperature of the LIB, the degradation mechanisms start to accumulate gases within the available volume within the LIB. Once the internal pressure reaches 20 bar, the NVLE phase starts. The accumulated gases evacuated the internal volume of the LIB until the internal pressure equilibrates the canister pressure (∼4.8 bar). From then, the internal pressure and canister pressure share the same value until TR occurs, in which the internal LIB pressure overcomes the canister pressure, allowing the rapid ejection of gases. First difference observed is the time-difference in between venting events, occurring at around 500 min for the NCA LIB, while at 790 min for the NMC LIB. Regarding the venting temperature, differences are observed with the experimental results. Simulation on NCA cell vents at a temperature of 109.6 °C, while reported experimental results indicate a mean venting temperature of 122.6 °C. Regarding NMC cells, the simulation returns a venting temperature of 131.4 °C, compared with the 125.6 °C of the average in experimental tests, showing in this case an overprediction of 5.8 °C, hence a lower deviation between methods, Fig. 13 . A second pressure rise occurs at the same time of TR event, with an increase of 1 bar for both cases. This pressure rise is associated with a sudden ejection of gases which, apart from the increase in temperature, cause an increase in the closed volume pressure.

The venting process in lithium-ion batteries involves the mechanical failure of a built-in venting valve within the battery casing. These valves are engineered to rupture at a specific pressure, typically ranging from 5 to 20 bars, depending on the battery type and manufacturer. Several studies have investigated the pressure at which venting occurs. For example, Garcia et al. examined LG18650MH1 cells and reported significant variability in the pressure required to open the venting cap, highlighting the complex nature of the phenomenon. This variability may help explain discrepancies between experimental and predicted venting temperatures in the present study. Notably, the model used here does not account for mechanical deformation and assumes a fixed venting pressure threshold of 20 bars.

The pressure characterization within the closed-volume also provides useful validation data for the predictive tool. Fig. 14 depicts this pressure evolution in the test-chamber. The two pressure-building mechanisms are properly differentiated. Prior to the separator collapse, the pressure rises due to the heating of the air and LIB within the volume, while when the Internal Short Circuit (ISC) takes place, a sudden amount of gaseous species are ejected, contributing to the rapid increase in pressure, and in a lower manner, the heating of the air due to the exhaust of hot gases. The low mass accumulated within the cell during VLE conditions do not see an effect on the pressure curves when the safety cap collapses. The CFD model in overall accurately predicts both pressure buildup mechanisms. Specifically, the rapid increase in pressure during TR captured through the computational model falls within the experimental standard deviation, hence indicates the appropriate replication of the mass flow rate of the venting of gases, independently of the chosen chemistry.

It results of high interest to assess when and at which characteristic temperatures the different gases are being generated and ejected. Therefore, the mass of the vented gases has been calculated for the simulations carried out, and they have been represented in Fig. 15 . Although the proportion between generated gases remain similar at the end of the thermal abuse procedure, the process to reach that final state differs between NCA and NMC811 chemistries. Prior to TR, CO2 is the main gas that is generated for both cases. For the NMC811 LIB, the amount of CO and CO2 generated prior to reaching TR stage increases progressively. On the other hand, in NCA cells there is no such increase, and once TR appears, a more abrupt ejection of gases takes place. The total mass of gases ejected is determined by the experimentally obtained mass loss, which has been assumed to be analogous. It is highlighted that the appearance of H2 is only produced when the binder reacts, producing a large proportion from the final gas distribution.

From these obtained ejected masses, the total amount of moles has been calculated, resulting in a molar weight of 8.24 g/mol and 8.55 g/mol for the NMC and NCA case respectively, indicating that a total of 0.3 and 0.41 mol of gas have been ejected from the cell during TR. According to literature, NMC 18,650 cells can emit up to 0.27 mol of gas [58] during a TR process. On the other hand, NCA cells have shown to emit a larger amount of gases, being reported by Golubkov et al. [57] to 0.31 mol, although other authors [58] report a maximum of 0.4 mol for NCA cells (considering maximum cell voltage). In this particular case, NMC cells lie within reported data, while NCA results emit more gases than reported, although with a small difference.

The thermal runaway (TR) process in lithium-ion batteries involves complex and highly dynamic phenomena that pose significant safety challenges. This research has advanced the understanding of TR by applying the Heat-Wait-Search (HWS) methodology within a closed-volume canister and an Accelerating Rate Calorimeter (ARC). The developed models integrate multiphysics simulations to capture both heat transfer and kinetic reactions occurring during TR events. These models successfully replicate experimental results, enabling the creation of a digital twin of the closed-volume system. Based on this study, the following conclusions can be drawn:
•
NCA cells reach thermal runaway slightly earlier than NMC811 cells, with onset temperatures averaging 128.9 °C for NCA and 130.6 °C for NMC811. Both cell types reach similar maximum temperatures during the thermal runaway, with NCA averaging 654.3 °C and NMC811 averaging 670.3 °C.
•
The gas analysis indicates that NMC811 cells produce a higher fraction of CO2 than CO. Additionally, NMC811 cells release 66 % more hydrocarbons than NCA cells.
•
The proposed CFD model allows calibration of TR kinetic model parameters to the experimentally-tested LIB. In the case of the NMC811 cell, the Ren et al. mechanism has been employed to adjust the experimental heat profile.
•
CFD results provide accurate replication of the LIB temperature with time, as well as the amount of vented gases, as the pressure build-up within the closed-volume chamber is properly captured for both simulated LIB chemistries.
•
Safety vent collapse is predicted at lower temperatures for NCA cell, resulting in deviations of 10.6 %, while for NMC811, a 4.6 % deviation is found. This is translated in differences in time regarding to that event.
•
The CFD approach allows assessing the process of the generation of gaseous products. Both chemistries show a distinct generation of CO2 in the phases prior to TR, while the remaining gaseous products suddenly appear once the highly exothermic process takes place.
•
The model allows extracting useful information for real-life applications, such as the TR trigger temperature, or the temperature at which the cell becomes exothermic, to prevent whether a TR event is about to happen, or if the chosen cooling system is enough to prevent propagation of TR to adjacent cells.

Future research may focus on automating the HWS procedure by applying CFD boundary conditions directly to the ARC walls rather than the closed-volume chamber, allowing for a more detailed integration with experimental data. While this study was conducted under inert conditions, future simulations should incorporate combustion processes, which are integral to TR scenarios, to fully represent ARCHWS behavior. Additionally, introducing internal thermocouples in experimental setups could improve thermal characterization during abuse testing, aiding in the calibration and validation of kinetic models.

Antonio García: Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization. Javier Monsalve-Serrano: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Javier Marco-Gimeno: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Carlos Guaraco-Figueira: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis.
